body:
Keeping your garage door tracks from sticking is mostly about **cleanliness, correct (minimal) lubrication, and alignment** —not actually greasing the tracks themselves.

Here’s a practical maintenance routine that works:

### 1\. Keep the tracks clean (this is the big one)

Dirt, old grease, and dust are what usually cause sticking—not lack of lubrication.

  * Wipe the inside of both vertical tracks with a dry or slightly damp cloth

  * Use a soft brush or vacuum to remove cobwebs, grit, and debris

  * If there’s hardened buildup, lightly scrub it off (a mild degreaser on a rag can help)

  * Dry the tracks completely afterward

Clean tracks let the rollers move freely without dragging or binding [Garage Door Replacement Parts Guide | Clopay](https://www.clopaydoor.com/residential/buyingguide/service-and-replacement?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### 2\. **Do NOT lubricate the tracks**

This is the most common mistake.

  * Tracks are meant to be dry guide rails

  * Grease or spray inside them attracts dirt and turns into sticky grime

  * That buildup causes sticking, jerky movement, and even derailment

Multiple maintenance guides specifically warn against lubricating tracks for this reason [Should You Lubricate a Garage Door Track? - Engineer Fix](https://engineerfix.com/should-you-lubricate-a-garage-door-track/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)[How To Lubricate A Garage Door | Utah Guide (2026)](https://advanced-door.com/how-to-lubricate-garage-door-guide/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### 3\. Lubricate the _right_ parts instead

Instead of the tracks, focus on the moving metal parts:

  * Roller bearings (not the track surface)

  * Hinge pivot points between door panels

  * Spring system (torsion or extension springs)

  * Bearing plates at the ends of the shaft

Use a **silicone spray or white lithium grease** —light application only [How to Lubricate Garage Door Tracks and Rollers – Dan's Garage Door Services](https://dansgaragedoorservices.com/lubricate-garage-door-tracks-rollers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### 4\. Check track alignment and hardware tightness

Even clean tracks can cause sticking if something is off:

  * Look for bent track sections

  * Tighten loose bolts/brackets on the track mounts

  * Make sure the tracks are parallel and evenly spaced from the door

Small misalignments can cause rollers to bind in certain spots.

### 5\. Inspect rollers (often the hidden cause)

If sticking keeps happening:

  * Worn or cracked rollers can drag in the track

  * Metal rollers may need lubrication at the bearings

  * Old plastic rollers often need replacement rather than lubrication

### 6\. Maintenance timing

  * Light cleaning: every 1–2 months if dusty

  * Full inspection/lube of moving parts: about every 6–12 months

* * *

If you want, I can walk you through diagnosing whether your issue is **dirty tracks vs. misalignment vs. worn rollers** based on what your door is doing (noise, sticking point, or uneven movement).
